title: Merck to Acquire Terns Pharmaceuticals, Inc., Expanding Its Hematology Pipeline With TERN-701, a Novel Candidate for Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML)
content:
Terns’ lead candidate TERN-701 is an investigational oral allosteric BCR::ABL1 tyrosine kinase inhibitor currently in Phase 1/2 development for certain patients with CML

Merck to hold investor call at 8 a.m. EDT today

RAHWAY, N.J. and FOSTER CITY, Calif., March  25, 2026  (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Merck (NYSE: MRK), known as MSD outside of the United States and Canada, and Terns Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (“Terns”) (Nasdaq: TERN), a clinical-stage oncology company, today announced that the companies have entered into a definitive agreement under which Merck, through a subsidiary, will acquire Terns for $53.00 per share in cash for an approximate equity value of $6.7 billion. This equates to approximately $5.7 billion net of acquired cash and represents an approximate premium of 31% to the 60-day and 42% to the 90-day volume-weighted average stock price on March 24, 2026.

“The acquisition of Terns builds on our growing presence in hematology with TERN-701, a potential best-in-class candidate for the treatment of certain patients with chronic myeloid leukemia,” said Robert M. Davis, chairman and chief executive officer, Merck. “This transaction further diversifies and strengthens our position in oncology as we continue to look for opportunities to broaden our portfolio into other therapeutic areas.”

Terns’ lead candidate, TERN-701, is a novel investigational oral allosteric BCR::ABL1 tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) currently being evaluated in the Phase 1/2 CARDINAL trial (NCT06163430) for patients with Philadelphia chromosome-positive (Ph+), chronic phase chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) previously treated with at least one prior TKI and who experienced treatment failure, suboptimal response or treatment intolerance. In March 2024, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) granted Orphan Drug Designation for TERN-701 for the treatment of CML.

“This acquisition reflects our team’s deep commitment to innovation in oncology and developing high impact medicines,” said Amy Burroughs, chief executive officer, Terns. “By working together, we will advance TERN-701, leveraging the deep expertise and significant resources at Merck, a global biopharmaceutical leader with a proven track record of delivering cancer breakthroughs for patients who need them most. I am immensely proud of the Terns team and our work towards making a difference for people living with CML. Finally, we extend our heartfelt thanks to the investigators, patients, and community advocates whose dedication and support make the development of TERN-701 possible.”

In clinical trials to date, TERN-701 has shown promising activity, with encouraging rates of major molecular response and deep molecular response observed by week 24. Importantly, this includes responses in patients with high disease burden who previously received multiple lines of therapy, including many who were treated with an allosteric TKI. The majority of treatment-emergent adverse events were reported as low grade with a low incidence of severe adverse events and discontinuations. No clinically meaningful changes in blood pressure have been observed, and rates of lipase elevation have been low.

“The first approval of a BCR::ABL1 tyrosine kinase inhibitor 25 years ago transformed the prognosis for many patients with chronic myeloid leukemia. Despite new therapeutic options, there is significant need for innovative, well-tolerated therapies with faster time to onset of molecular response leading to deeper responses and better disease control,” said Dr. Dean Y. Li, president, Merck Research Laboratories. “Based on early clinical evidence, TERN-701, a novel allosteric BCR::ABL1 inhibitor, may have the potential to provide a meaningfully differentiated option for certain patients living with CML.”

The transaction has been approved by both Merck’s and Terns’ Boards of Directors. Under the terms of the merger agreement, Merck, through a subsidiary, will acquire all of the outstanding shares of Terns. The acquisition is subject to a majority of Terns’ stockholders tendering their shares in a tender offer that will be initiated by a subsidiary of Merck. The closing of the proposed transaction will be subject to certain conditions, including the expiration of the waiting period under the Hart-Scott-Rodino Antitrust Improvements Act and other customary conditions. The transaction is expected to be accounted for as an asset acquisition and close in the second quarter of 2026, resulting in a charge of approximately $5.8 billion, or approximately $2.35 per share, included in both second quarter and full year 2026 GAAP and non-GAAP results.

A copy of the merger agreement for the transaction will be filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission (“SEC”) and will be publicly available free of charge at the SEC’s website at www.sec.gov. Copies of the documents filed with the SEC by Merck may be obtained at no charge from Merck’s website at www.merck.com or by contacting Merck at 126 East Lincoln Avenue, P.O. Box 2000, Rahway, NJ 07065 USA, or (908) 740-4000. Copies of the documents filed with the SEC by Terns may be obtained at no charge from Terns’ website at www.ternspharma.comor by contacting Terns at 1065 East Hillsdale Blvd., Suite 100, Foster City, CA 94404 or (650) 525-5535 Ext.101.

Investor Call
Merck will hold an investor call Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at 8 a.m. EDT to discuss the proposed transaction. Journalists who wish to ask questions should contact a member of Merck’s Global Media Relations team at the conclusion of the call. Investors, journalists and the general public may access a live audio webcast of the call via this weblink.

All participants may join the call by dialing (800) 369-2154 (U.S. and Canada Toll-Free) or (517) 308-9422 and using the access code 8711041.

Advisors
Centerview Partners LLC and Jefferies LLC acted as financial advisors to Terns and Freshfields LLP acted as Terns’ legal advisor.

About TERN-701
TERN-701 is a novel investigational oral allosteric BCR::ABL1 tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) designed to bind to the ABL myristoyl pocket, with a potentially best-in-disease profile that could improve upon the efficacy, safety and convenience of existing treatments for CML.

About the CARDINAL study
TERN-701 is currently being evaluated in the CARDINAL trial (NCT06163430), a global multi-center dose escalation and dose-expansion clinical trial to assess safety, tolerability and efficacy in patients with Philadelphia chromosome-positive (Ph+) chronic phase CML previously treated with at least one prior TKI and experienced treatment failure, suboptimal response, or treatment intolerance. The dose escalation portion of the CARDINAL trial completed in January 2025 with no dose limiting toxicities observed up to the maximum dose of 500mg QD. Terns initiated the dose expansion portion of the trial in April 2025 with patients randomized to one of two dose cohorts (320mg or 500mg QD) with up to 40 patients per arm. In January 2026, an additional cohort was added to the CARDINAL trial to evaluate TERN-701 500 mg QD in approximately 20 patients with BCR::ABL1 resistance mutations including T315I, M244V, F359I/C/V and others.

About chronic myeloid leukemia
Chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) is a slow growing type of blood cancer that leads to an overproduction of white blood cells that accumulate in the blood and bone marrow, disrupting the production of healthy blood cells. CML is commonly associated with the Philadelphia chromosome, a translocation between chromosomes 9 and 22 that results in constitutive activation of the BCR::ABL1 fusion protein, which fuels cancer growth.

About Merck in hematology
Merck is advancing a pipeline of hematology candidates targeting a diverse range of targets across leukemias, lymphomas and myeloproliferative neoplasms. Candidates in Phase 3 development include: bomedemstat (MK-3543), an investigational, orally available lysine-specific demethylase 1 (LSD1) inhibitor; nemtabrutinib (MK-1026), an investigational, non-covalent Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitor; and zilovertamab vedotin (MK-2140), an investigational antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) that targets receptor tyrosine kinase-like orphan receptor 1 (ROR1). Additionally, MK-1045, an investigational CD19xCD3 T-cell engager, is currently being evaluated in a Phase 1b/2 trial.

About Terns Pharmaceuticals
Terns Pharmaceuticals is a clinical-stage oncology company reimagining known biology to deliver high impact medicines. Terns’ lead program, TERN-701, is a highly selective, oral, allosteric BCR::ABL1 inhibitor with a potentially best-in-disease profile that could meaningfully improve upon the efficacy, safety and convenience of existing treatments for chronic myeloid leukemia. For more information, please visit: www.ternspharma.com.

About Merck
At Merck, known as MSD outside of the United States and Canada, we are unified around our purpose: We use the power of leading-edge science to save and improve lives around the world. For more than 130 years, we have brought hope to humanity through the development of important medicines and vaccines. We aspire to be the premier research-intensive biopharmaceutical company in the world – and today, we are at the forefront of research to deliver innovative health solutions that advance the prevention and treatment of diseases in people and animals. We foster a diverse and inclusive global workforce and operate responsibly every day to enable a safe, sustainable and healthy future for all people and communities. For more information, visit www.merck.com and connect with us on X (formerly Twitter), Facebook, Instagram, YouTube and LinkedIn.

title: Merck to Acquire Terns Pharmaceuticals, Inc., Expanding Its Hematology Pipeline With TERN-701, a Novel Candidate for Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML)
content:
Terns Pharmaceuticals, Inc.

Terns’ lead candidate TERN-701 is an investigational oral allosteric BCR::ABL1 tyrosine kinase inhibitor currently in Phase 1/2 development for certain patients with CML

Merck to hold investor call at 8 a.m. EDT today

RAHWAY, N.J. and FOSTER CITY, Calif., March 25, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Merck (NYSE: MRK), known as MSD outside of the United States and Canada, and Terns Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (“Terns”) (Nasdaq: TERN), a clinical-stage oncology company, today announced that the companies have entered into a definitive agreement under which Merck, through a subsidiary, will acquire Terns for $53.00 per share in cash for an approximate equity value of $6.7 billion. This equates to approximately $5.7 billion net of acquired cash and represents an approximate premium of 31% to the 60-day and 42% to the 90-day volume-weighted average stock price on March 24, 2026.

“The acquisition of Terns builds on our growing presence in hematology with TERN-701, a potential best-in-class candidate for the treatment of certain patients with chronic myeloid leukemia,” said Robert M. Davis, chairman and chief executive officer, Merck. “This transaction further diversifies and strengthens our position in oncology as we continue to look for opportunities to broaden our portfolio into other therapeutic areas.”

Terns’ lead candidate, TERN-701, is a novel investigational oral allosteric BCR::ABL1 tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) currently being evaluated in the Phase 1/2 CARDINAL trial (NCT06163430) for patients with Philadelphia chromosome-positive (Ph+), chronic phase chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) previously treated with at least one prior TKI and who experienced treatment failure, suboptimal response or treatment intolerance. In March 2024, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) granted Orphan Drug Designation for TERN-701 for the treatment of CML.

“This acquisition reflects our team’s deep commitment to innovation in oncology and developing high impact medicines,” said Amy Burroughs, chief executive officer, Terns. “By working together, we will advance TERN-701, leveraging the deep expertise and significant resources at Merck, a global biopharmaceutical leader with a proven track record of delivering cancer breakthroughs for patients who need them most. I am immensely proud of the Terns team and our work towards making a difference for people living with CML. Finally, we extend our heartfelt thanks to the investigators, patients, and community advocates whose dedication and support make the development of TERN-701 possible.”

Story Continues

In clinical trials to date, TERN-701 has shown promising activity, with encouraging rates of major molecular response and deep molecular response observed by week 24. Importantly, this includes responses in patients with high disease burden who previously received multiple lines of therapy, including many who were treated with an allosteric TKI. The majority of treatment-emergent adverse events were reported as low grade with a low incidence of severe adverse events and discontinuations. No clinically meaningful changes in blood pressure have been observed, and rates of lipase elevation have been low.

“The first approval of a BCR::ABL1 tyrosine kinase inhibitor 25 years ago transformed the prognosis for many patients with chronic myeloid leukemia. Despite new therapeutic options, there is significant need for innovative, well-tolerated therapies with faster time to onset of molecular response leading to deeper responses and better disease control,” said Dr. Dean Y. Li, president, Merck Research Laboratories. “Based on early clinical evidence, TERN-701, a novel allosteric BCR::ABL1 inhibitor, may have the potential to provide a meaningfully differentiated option for certain patients living with CML.”

The transaction has been approved by both Merck’s and Terns’ Boards of Directors. Under the terms of the merger agreement, Merck, through a subsidiary, will acquire all of the outstanding shares of Terns. The acquisition is subject to a majority of Terns’ stockholders tendering their shares in a tender offer that will be initiated by a subsidiary of Merck. The closing of the proposed transaction will be subject to certain conditions, including the expiration of the waiting period under the Hart-Scott-Rodino Antitrust Improvements Act and other customary conditions. The transaction is expected to be accounted for as an asset acquisition and close in the second quarter of 2026, resulting in a charge of approximately $5.8 billion, or approximately $2.35 per share, included in both second quarter and full year 2026 GAAP and non-GAAP results.

A copy of the merger agreement for the transaction will be filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission (“SEC”) and will be publicly available free of charge at the SEC’s website at www.sec.gov. Copies of the documents filed with the SEC by Merck may be obtained at no charge from Merck’s website at www.merck.com or by contacting Merck at 126 East Lincoln Avenue, P.O. Box 2000, Rahway, NJ 07065 USA, or (908) 740-4000. Copies of the documents filed with the SEC by Terns may be obtained at no charge from Terns’ website at www.ternspharma.comor by contacting Terns at 1065 East Hillsdale Blvd., Suite 100, Foster City, CA 94404 or (650) 525-5535 Ext.101.

Investor Call
Merck will hold an investor call Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at 8 a.m. EDT to discuss the proposed transaction. Journalists who wish to ask questions should contact a member of Merck’s Global Media Relations team at the conclusion of the call. Investors, journalists and the general public may access a live audio webcast of the call via this weblink.

All participants may join the call by dialing (800) 369-2154 (U.S. and Canada Toll-Free) or (517) 308-9422 and using the access code 8711041.

Advisors
Centerview Partners LLC and Jefferies LLC acted as financial advisors to Terns and Freshfields LLP acted as Terns’ legal advisor.

About TERN-701
TERN-701 is a novel investigational oral allosteric BCR::ABL1 tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) designed to bind to the ABL myristoyl pocket, with a potentially best-in-disease profile that could improve upon the efficacy, safety and convenience of existing treatments for CML.

About the CARDINAL study
TERN-701 is currently being evaluated in the CARDINAL trial (NCT06163430), a global multi-center dose escalation and dose-expansion clinical trial to assess safety, tolerability and efficacy in patients with Philadelphia chromosome-positive (Ph+) chronic phase CML previously treated with at least one prior TKI and experienced treatment failure, suboptimal response, or treatment intolerance. CML is commonly associated with the Philadelphia chromosome, a translocation between chromosomes 9 and 22 that results in constitutive activation of the BCR::ABL1 fusion protein, which fuels cancer growth.

Additionally, MK-1045, an investigational CD19xCD3 T-cell engager, is currently being evaluated in a Phase 1b/2 trial.

About Terns Pharmaceuticals

For more information, please visit: www.ternspharma.com.

About Merck
At Merck, known as MSD outside of the United States and Canada, we are unified around our purpose: We use the power of leading-edge science to save and improve lives around the world. For more than 130 years, we have brought hope to humanity through the development of important medicines and vaccines. We aspire to be the premier research-intensive biopharmaceutical company in the world – and today, we are at the forefront of research to deliver innovative health solutions that advance the prevention and treatment of diseases in people and animals. We foster a diverse and inclusive global workforce and operate responsibly every day to enable a safe, sustainable and healthy future for all people and communities. For more information, visit www.merck.com and connect with us on X (formerly Twitter), Facebook, Instagram, YouTube and LinkedIn.

title: Merck close to $6B acquisition of Terns Pharma to boost cancer portfolio: FT
content:
[Merck & Co. headquarters in Silicon Valley]
Sundry Photography

Merck & Co. (MRK [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/MRK]) is close to an approximately $6 billion all-cash deal to buy US biotech firm Terns Pharmaceuticals (TERN [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/TERN]) as it seeks to boost its drug pipeline ahead of cancer treatment Keytruda’s patent expiry, the _Financial Times_ reported.

The talks are at an advanced stage and could be finalized in the coming days, the FT reported, citing people it didn’t identify. Terns (TERN [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/TERN]) had a $5.4 billion market capitalization as of Tuesday’s close.

Merck and Terns didn’t immediately respond to requests for comment from the FT.

Following ⁠the news, shares of Terns Pharma, which focuses on developing treatments for ⁠chronic ​myeloid leukemia, rose about ​10% in after-hours trading.

Merck (MRK [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/MRK]) has been pursuing deals to strengthen its pipeline, with CEO Rob Davis spearheading a strategy to diversify the company's portfolio ahead of the 2028 patent expiration for its blockbuster cancer drug, Keytruda.

content:

Merck (MRK) just signed a multi year research collaboration with Quotient Therapeutics focused on inflammatory bowel disease, built around Quotient’s somatic genomics platform and a milestone structure worth up to US$2.2b.

Quotient receives an upfront US$20 million payment, while Merck gains access to a technology that examines naturally occurring somatic mutations in patient tissues to identify targets that may drive or protect against IBD and other conditions.

See our latest analysis for Merck.

Merck’s collaboration with Quotient arrives after a series of recent updates, including new GARDASIL data and conference appearances, with the 90 day share price return of 9.32% and 1 year total shareholder return of 37.25% signaling firm, longer term momentum.

Merck & Co. presents a compelling long-term investment case, underpinned by its innovative capacity and strong market position. Read the complete narrative.

While the community narrative points to Merck being 3.4% overvalued at $116.37 versus a $112.55 fair value, our DCF model lands in a very different place, with a fair value estimate of $208.16 that suggests the shares are trading at a 44.1% discount. title: 1 S&P 500 Stock to Target This Week and 2 We Ignore
content:
The S&P 500 (^GSPC) is home to the biggest and most well-known companies in the market, making it a go-to index for investors seeking stability. But not all large-cap stocks are created equal - some are struggling with slowing growth, declining margins, or increased competition.

Picking the right S&P 500 stocks requires more than just buying big names, and that’s where StockStory comes in. That said, here is one S&P 500 stock that is leading the market forward and two that may struggle.

Two Stocks to Sell:

CVS Health (CVS)

Market Cap: $90.7 billion

With over 9,000 retail pharmacy locations serving as neighborhood health destinations across America, CVS Health (NYSE:CVS) operates retail pharmacies, provides pharmacy benefit management services, and offers health insurance through its Aetna subsidiary.

Why Do We Think Twice About CVS?

The company has faced growth challenges as its 6% annual revenue increases over the last two years fell short of other healthcare companies Sales are projected to remain flat over the next 12 months as demand decelerates from its two-year trend Incremental sales over the last five years were much less profitable as its earnings per share fell by 2.1% annually while its revenue grew

CVS Health is trading at $71.09 per share, or 10x forward P/E. Check out our free in-depth research report to learn more about why CVS doesn’t pass our bar.

PulteGroup (PHM)

Market Cap: $22.73 billion

Having delivered over 850,000 homes since its founding in 1950, PulteGroup (NYSE:PHM) is one of America's largest homebuilders, constructing single-family homes, townhouses, and condominiums for first-time, move-up, and active adult buyers across 46 markets in 25 states.

Why Are We Cautious About PHM?

The company has faced growth challenges as its 3.8% annual revenue increases over the last two years fell short of other industrials companies Earnings per share have dipped by 1.4% annually over the past two years, which is concerning because stock prices follow EPS over the long term Diminishing returns on capital suggest its earlier profit pools are drying up

PulteGroup’s stock price of $118.64 implies a valuation ratio of 11.2x forward P/E. Read our free research report to see why you should think twice about including PHM in your portfolio, it’s free.

One Stock to Buy:

Merck (MRK)

Market Cap: $286 billion

With roots dating back to 1891 and a portfolio that includes the blockbuster cancer immunotherapy Keytruda, Merck (NYSE:MRK) develops and sells prescription medicines, vaccines, and animal health products across oncology, infectious diseases, cardiovascular, and other therapeutic areas.

Story Continues

Why Are We Backing MRK?

Dominant market position is represented by its $65.09 billion in revenue, which creates significant barriers to entry in this highly regulated industry Adjusted operating margin improvement of 30.8 percentage points over the last two years demonstrates its ability to scale efficiently MRK is a free cash flow machine with the flexibility to invest in growth initiatives or return capital to shareholders

At $115.34 per share, Merck trades at 22.1x forward P/E. Is now the right time to buy? See for yourself in our full research report, it’s free.

Stocks We Like Even More

ALSO WORTH WATCHING: Top 5 Momentum Stocks. The best time to own a great stock is when the market is finally noticing it. These aren't just high-quality businesses. Something is happening with them right now. Elite fundamentals meeting near-term momentum — both boxes checked at the same time.

Find out which stocks our AI platform is flagging this week. See this week's Strong Momentum stocks — FREE.  Get Our Strong Momentum Stocks for Free HERE.

title: Conductor Introduces AEO Trailblazer Awards, Recognizing Enterprise Brands Leading in AI Search Visibility
content:
NEW YORK, March 24, 2026--(BUSINESS WIRE)--Conductor, the only end-to-end enterprise AEO platform, today announced the winners of its inaugural AEO Trailblazer Awards, recognizing enterprise brands that are redefining how visibility is earned in AI-powered search.

Search has changed. AI answers increasingly shape how customers discover, evaluate, and trust brands before a click ever happens. AI referral traffic may still be early, but its influence is outsized, shaping how brands are perceived and shortlisted before customers ever reach a website. The organizations honored today saw that shift early and translated Answer Engine Optimization (AEO) into real, measurable impact. They moved beyond experimentation and made AEO a repeatable, enterprise capability.

Leading global brands across industries were recognized for translating AEO from concept into enterprise execution, including organizations such as Airbnb, EY, Mastercard, Sonos, and Merck. While their approaches vary, these teams share a common focus: strengthening how their brands are surfaced, cited, and trusted within AI-generated answers.

Across this year’s winners, AEO is no longer confined to SEO or content teams. It is becoming a cross-functional capability, spanning marketing, digital experience, and broader business strategy. Some organizations are advancing large-scale content and distribution strategies to increase share of voice across AI platforms, while others are applying AEO insights to inform decisions around brand positioning, policy, and reputation.

Together, these leaders reflect a broader shift in the market: from isolated experimentation to coordinated, enterprise-wide efforts that treat AI visibility as a core driver of digital growth.

View the full list of 2026 AEO Trailblazer Award winners here: https://www.conductor.com/lp/aeo-trailblazer-awards/

These teams stood out not for isolated tactics but for leadership. They aligned strategy across marketing and digital functions, invested with conviction, and delivered tangible progress in how their brands appear, are cited, and are represented inside AI-generated answers. Their work reflects maturity in a space where many are still experimenting.

"The brands recognized in this year’s AEO Trailblazer Awards are not just adopting AEO, they are operationalizing it," said Seth Besmertnik, CEO and Co-founder of Conductor. "They understood early that AI visibility would become foundational to digital growth, and they built the systems and accountability to earn it consistently. This inaugural class sets the standard for what strong AEO looks like in the enterprise."

Story Continues

The AEO Trailblazer Awards expand Conductor’s long-standing tradition of recognizing industry leadership and customer excellence. This new annual award series reflects the growing strategic importance of AI visibility as a core performance channel for modern marketing organizations.

As AI continues to reshape the digital landscape, the companies recognized today offer a clear signal to the market. Real AEO success is not about chasing headlines. It is about building systems that earn consistent visibility inside AI answers, where decisions are increasingly made.

About the AEO Trailblazer Awards

The AEO Trailblazer Awards recognize enterprises that are operationalizing Answer Engine Optimization and turning emerging strategy into measurable impact. Winners are selected based on demonstrated maturity, leadership, and outcomes that signal credibility in the evolving AI search landscape.

Learn more about the AEO Trailblazer Awards from Conductor here.

About Conductor

Conductor is the only end-to-end, enterprise AEO platform built on the industry’s most complete data engine. The unified platform provides unparalleled AEO & SEO intelligence, scaled AI content generation, and real-time website monitoring. From tracking visibility in LLMs to technical health and content optimization, Conductor’s platform empowers teams to increase authority and visibility in AI and traditional search and accelerate digital growth. Conductor was named a Leader in the Forrester Wave for SEO platforms and is rated #1 on TrustRadius and G2 by enterprise marketers. Customers include global brands such as Citi, e.l.f Cosmetics, & Mastercard. Learn more: conductor.com.

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260324779226/en/

title: New Public Listings for Canadians Living with Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension
content:
KIRKLAND, QC, March 24, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Merck (NYSE: MRK), known as MSD outside of the United States and Canada, announced today that British Columbia, Non-Insured Health Benefits (NIHB), Nova Scotia, Quebec and Veterans Affairs Canada (VAC) have added WINREVAIR® (sotatercept) to their public drug formularies. As the first jurisdictions to implement public coverage following the completed pan-Canadian Pharmaceutical Alliance (pCPA) agreement, these listings mark an important step toward improving access for Canadians living with pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH), a rare, progressive and life‑limiting disease.Merck Logo (PRNewsfoto/Merck)

WINREVAIR® is indicated in combination with standard pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) therapy, for the treatment of adults with World Health Organization [WHO] Group 1 PAH and Functional Class (FC) II, III or IV.

"Today's announcement represents meaningful progress for Canadians affected by PAH," said Jamie Myrah, Executive Director at Pulmonary Hypertension Association of Canada. "PAH is a debilitating and progressive disease that places a significant physical, emotional and financial strain not only on patients, but also on the families and caregivers who support them. Increasing access to treatment options is needed to help these patients. When people living with PAH have more options, the benefits extend beyond patients to their loved ones and the broader community."

"We are encouraged to see WINREVAIR® now listed in five public programs," said David Jones, Managing Director, Merck Canada. "This is an important step toward expanding access to therapies for people living with PAH, and we remain committed to working collaboratively with remaining jurisdictions to ensure timely access for all eligible Canadian patients."

About pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) PAH is a serious, progressive condition in which the small arteries that carry blood from the heart to the lungs become narrowed by excess cell growth and scarring. That narrowing reduces blood flow to the lungs and lowers oxygen levels in the blood. PAH can be idiopathic, inherited, or linked to other conditions. Common symptoms include increasing shortness of breath (especially with exertion), fatigue, swelling of the feet and legs, chest pain, dizziness and fainting — symptoms that often develop slowly and can be mistaken for deconditioning or other heart and lung problems. Left untreated, PAH places strain on the heart, which can lead to reduced mobility, heart failure and reduced life expectancy.

Story Continues

About Merck At Merck, known as MSD outside of the United States and Canada, we are unified around our purpose: We use the power of leading-edge science to save and improve lives around the world. For more than 130 years, we have brought hope to humanity through the development of important medicines and vaccines. We aspire to be the premier research-intensive biopharmaceutical company in the world – and today, we are at the forefront of research to deliver innovative health solutions that advance the prevention and treatment of diseases in people and animals. We foster a diverse and inclusive global workforce and operate responsibly every day to enable a safe, sustainable, and healthy future for all people and communities. For more information about our operations in Canada, visit www.merck.ca and connect with us on LinkedIn @MerckCanada.

Forward-Looking Statement of Merck & Co., Inc., Rahway, N.J., USA This news release of Merck & Co., Inc., Rahway, N.J., USA (the "company") includes "forward-looking statements" within the meaning of the safe harbor provisions of the U.S. Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995. Risks and uncertainties include but are not limited to, general industry conditions and competition; general economic factors, including interest rate and currency exchange rate fluctuations; the impact of pharmaceutical industry regulation and health care legislation in the United States and internationally; global trends toward health care cost containment; technological advances, new products and patents attained by competitors; challenges inherent in new product development, including obtaining regulatory approval; the company's ability to accurately predict future market conditions; manufacturing difficulties or delays; financial instability of international economies and sovereign risk; dependence on the effectiveness of the company's patents and other protections for innovative products; and the exposure to litigation, including patent litigation, and/or regulatory actions.

title: Organon: A High Cash Yield Hidden Behind Patent Fatigue
content:
This article first appeared on GuruFocus.

Introduction

Organon is an American pharmaceutical company spun off from Merck & Co. in 2021 with a focus on women's health, biosimilars, and established brands. Headquartered in Jersey City, it markets a portfolio that includes contraceptives such as Nexplanon, fertility treatments, and biosimilar therapeutics designed to provide cost-effective alternatives to branded biologics. Nearly a third of the company's revenue is derived from the United States, with the balance coming from global markets where it sells products in reproductive medicine, hormone therapies, and other therapeutic categories.

Warning! GuruFocus has detected 7 Warning Signs with OGN. List of 52-Week Lows List of 3-Year Lows List of 5-Year Lows Is OGN fairly valued? Test your thesis with our free DCF calculator.

In its most recent full-year results, Organon reported revenue of approximately $6.2 billion for 2025, a decline of about 3% from the prior year on both an as-reported and constant currency basis. Adjusted EBITDA for the year was roughly $1.9 billion, yielding a margin north of 30% on that measure. Management has guided 2026 revenue and EBITDA to be broadly flat with 2025, reflecting expectations for continued operational stability without meaningful top-line growth.

The fourth quarter 2025 results highlighted both the strengths and headwinds into which the company is navigating. Total quarterly revenue was down year-over-year, with the women's health segment declining and biosimilars growing modestly, driven in part by strong performance of Hadlima and contributions from newly approved products. Non-GAAP adjusted gross margins narrowed versus the prior period, and the quarter produced a net loss primarily due to a non-cash goodwill impairment associated with underperformance in select product lines.

Organon's leadership and governance recently saw a notable shift: the CEO, Kevin Ali, resigned following an internal investigation into past sales practices involving its Nexplanon implant, prompting a focus on strengthening internal controls and financial reporting procedures. Joseph Morrissey, the company's head of manufacturing and supply, was appointed interim CEO. While the board described the financial impact of those practices as small relative to overall company revenue, the incident influenced investor sentiment and share performance.

These developments frame the current context in which the company operates: a mature portfolio with solid cash generation, pressures from patent expirations and pricing, and an active management agenda focused on deleveraging and control improvements. The recent results, leadership transition, and flat guidance for 2026 together set the baseline for a valuation and owner-return analysis in the sections that follow.

Story Continues

The Economic Engine

The business is best understood as three cash-generating portfolios that behave very differently over a cycle, but all roll up into the same economic question: how much of today's cash flow is durable, and how much is slowly melting.

In 2025, Organon generated $6.216 billion of revenue. Established Brands remained the center of gravity at $3.691 billion, Women's Health contributed $1.752 billion, and Biosimilars added $691 million. The overall top line declined 3% year over year, but that headline masks a more important distinction: Women's Health and Biosimilars are the parts you can still underwrite as managed franchises, while Established Brands is the part you underwrite as an amortizing asset that must be harvested carefully.

Women's Health is not a single-product story, but it does have a single product that dominates the investor narrative. In 2025, the company described growth in its fertility business and the JADA system as meaningful offsets to declines elsewhere, but it also disclosed that Nexplanon fell 4% ex-FX for the full year. The U.S. was the weak point: Organon noted a 9% U.S. decline tied to policy-related access restrictions beginning in early 2025, partially offset by stronger performance outside the U.S. This matters because it tells you the durability of Women's Health is not purely clinical demand; it is also reimbursement and access plumbing. Organon also disclosed that JADA delivered $74 million of revenue in 2025, and then divested the JADA system on January 28, 2026. That sale reduces a small but real growth offset going forward, and it forces the rest of the portfolio to carry more of the stability burden.

Biosimilars is the only portfolio that grew in 2025, up 4% as reported and 5% ex-FX, driven primarily by Hadlima. The company also pointed to contributions from recently added or newly approved assets, including Bildyos and Bilprevda (denosumab-nxxp) and Tofidence, which it acquired in 2025. At the same time, this portfolio is not immune to the commoditizing logic of biosimilars. Organon explicitly described Renflexis and Ontruzant as being in the mature phase of their life cycles, with significant competitive pricing pressure in the U.S. and Europe. The economic takeaway is that biosimilars can be a growth bridge, but not a high-margin annuity. The underwriting question is whether the company can keep refreshing the portfolio faster than price compression erodes the older molecules.

Established Brands is where the cash comes from, and also where the decay risk lives. Revenue declined 4% as reported (5% ex-FX) in 2025. Organon pointed to contributions from Emgality and Vtama as partial offsets, while calling out declines in its respiratory portfolio and the impact from loss of exclusivity for Atozet in Europe and Japan. In the fourth quarter commentary, the company also linked respiratory weakness to pricing pressure and to guideline changes that deprioritize montelukast in several international markets, which contributed to volume declines. That is the shape of the problem here: mature products are not just subject to generic competition; they can also be structurally impaired by shifts in clinical practice and payer behavior.

Despite these cross-currents, profitability remained resilient on an adjusted basis. For 2025, Organon reported an adjusted gross margin of 60.1% and an adjusted EBITDA margin of 30.7%, with adjusted EBITDA of $1.907 billion. Reported gross margin fell to 53.3% as the company cited one-time costs tied to optimizing its manufacturing and supply network, along with amortization and acquisition-related costs connected to the Dermavant acquisition completed in October 2024. The key point for an owner is that this is still a high cash-producing platform, but it is a platform whose cash flows are the result of disciplined harvesting plus selective reinvestment, not a portfolio that can simply grow its way out of leverage.

Capital Structure and Capital Allocation

Organon's operating profile reads like a cash-generative franchise set. Its capital structure reads like a leveraged harvest vehicle. As of Dec. 31, 2025, the company reported $8.64 billion of total debt and $574 million of cash and cash equivalents. With 2025 adjusted EBITDA of $1.907 billion, net leverage sits a little above 4x on management's preferred denominator. In that range, equity holders do not primarily own earnings. They own the residual after interest, taxes and the cash required to keep the portfolio from decaying faster than the balance sheet can be repaired.

What makes this credit manageable, and investable for the right kind of owner, is time. The contractual maturity schedule is front-loaded in a way that creates a single, visible wall rather than a series of near-term traps. At year-end 2025, Organon's disclosed principal maturities were roughly: $10 million in 2026, $10 million in 2027, $3.58 billion in 2028, $10 million in 2029, $21 million in 2030, and $5.07 billion thereafter. In other words, the constraint is not next quarter. It is the 2028 refinancing moment, and whether the company can walk into that window with meaningfully lower net debt and cleaner governance.

The cash engine is real, but the capital stack gets first call on it. In 2025, Organon generated $700 million of net cash provided by operating activities and spent $162 million on capital expenditures. That leaves a baseline free-cash profile that is substantial relative to the equity value the market has assigned, but not unlimited relative to the debt it has to amortize and refinance. The company's financing cash flows show how management is behaving under that constraint: 2025 included $1.513 billion of debt repayments against $1.055 billion of debt proceeds, and management cited the repurchase and cancellation of $419 million of its 2031 notes as a meaningful deleveraging action during the year. The dividend has also been intentionally reduced to a token level, the board declared a $0.02 quarterly dividend in February 2026, consistent with the company's post-2025 posture that cash belongs with debt reduction before anything else.

The cleanest capital allocation event around the turn of the year was the divestiture of the JADA system. Organon agreed to sell JADA for up to $465 million, including $440 million payable at closing (subject to customary adjustments) and a potential earn-out tied to 2026 revenue targets; the company has stated that net proceeds are intended for debt reduction, and it closed the transaction on Jan. 28, 2026. In isolation, that does not fix leverage. But it matters because it clarifies priorities: management is willing to sell a growth asset if it accelerates the balance-sheet path. For an owner underwriting a levered equity in a mixed-durability portfolio, that trade is directionally correct.

The underwriting question in this section is therefore not whether the company can generate cash in a single year. It already does. The question is whether it can keep the cash base intact long enough to shrink net debt materially before the 2028 wall. If sustainable free cash flow lands in the $600700 million neighborhood (the range implied by recent results and management's emphasis on cash durability), the math becomes straightforward: over three to four years, the business has a plausible line of sight to retire a meaningful portion of net debt even without growth, and that time value is part of the equity case. If operating erosion accelerates (Established Brands decays faster, or Women's Health faces persistent access pressure), the same maturity wall turns from time into terms, and refinancing becomes the mechanism by which value can leak out of the equityOrganon: A High Cash Yield Hidden Behind Patent Fatigue

Debt Reduction Capacity Before the Maturity Wall

The maturity schedule is only half the story. The other half is the company's ability to reduce principal before the refinancing window arrives. Organon's recent results suggest that the free cash flow base, while not immune to portfolio erosion, remains substantial relative to the size of the debt stack. In 2025, the company reported approximately $700 million of net cash provided by operating activities and spent $162 million on capital expenditures. That places normalized free cash flow comfortably in the several-hundred-million-dollar range even after interest, taxes, and maintenance investment.

If one assumes a sustainable free cash flow range of roughly $600 million to $700 million annually, a level broadly consistent with the company's recent cash generation, the arithmetic becomes straightforward. Over the three-year window leading up to the 2028 maturity wall, that implies cumulative debt reduction capacity of approximately $1.8 billion to $2.1 billion before any refinancing is required. This estimate assumes the continuation of the current capital allocation posture: a minimal dividend, limited discretionary acquisitions, and a primary focus on balance sheet repair.

That scale of potential deleveraging does not eliminate leverage risk, but it materially changes the timing of it. Entering the 2028 refinancing cycle with net debt reduced by roughly $2 billion would lower leverage ratios and reduce the amount of capital that must be rolled in a single transaction. In other words, the key asset embedded in Organon's capital structure is not growth, it is time combined with cash generation. The company does not need heroic operational improvement to create equity value; it needs the existing cash flow base to remain intact long enough for the balance sheet to shrink.

This dynamic also explains why management's recent actions have focused so heavily on debt reduction. The sale of the JADA system, completed in January 2026 for $440 million upfront with additional potential earn-out consideration, is a good example. In isolation, the transaction does not transform the capital structure. In context, it accelerates the timeline by which net debt can move meaningfully lower. For an owner evaluating a leveraged equity claim, that acceleration matters more than incremental top-line growth.

Valuation

The cleanest way to value Organon is to treat it as a capital-structure problem first and a portfolio problem second. The equity is small relative to the enterprise, so modest changes in cash flow durability or leverage do not move the debt much, they move the equity a lot.

Start with what the company actually produced in 2025 and what management is telling you to assume for 2026. Organon reported 2025 revenue of $6.216 billion and adjusted EBITDA of $1.907 billion. Management guided 2026 revenue and adjusted EBITDA to be broadly flat, which effectively frames the base case as hold the line rather than grow out of leverage. At year-end 2025, the business carried $8.64 billion of total debt and $574 million of cash, leaving net leverage around the low-4x range on adjusted EBITDA. That leverage is not an abstract risk factor; it is the mechanism that determines where returns accrue.

At the prevailing equity price in mid-February 2026, the market capitalization was roughly $2 billion. Add net debt and the enterprise value lands around $10 billion, which implies an EV/EBITDA multiple in the mid-5x to mid-6x range on trailing adjusted EBITDA. That is a compressed multiple for a company still producing north of $1.9 billion of adjusted EBITDA, and the market's message is straightforward: this is an amortizing portfolio with leverage, not a franchise that deserves a normal pharma multiple.

The more useful lens is the capital stack spread. In 2025, Organon disclosed cash interest expense in the mid-$400 million range, cash taxes in the high-$200 million range, and capital expenditures around $160 million. Even after one-time items, the company reported free cash flow of roughly $659 million for 2025. Put differently, the debt sits in front of an annual cash stream that is large enough to matter, and the equity sits behind it with a market value that implies a very high free-cash-flow yield. That is exactly why the stock can look optically cheap and still be risky: if the cash stream erodes, the equity absorbs the damage first; if the cash stream holds and leverage comes down, the equity captures the convexity that bondholders do not.Organon: A High Cash Yield Hidden Behind Patent Fatigue

This is the underwriting bridge I care about under a no-rerating assumption. If free cash flow can be sustained in a ~$600$700 million band and most of it is directed to debt reduction, the balance sheet can de-risk meaningfully over a three- to four-year window. Even if the enterprise multiple never improves, the equity can compound simply because the debt claim shrinks while the enterprise cash flow holds. The flip side is also true: if Established Brands decay accelerates or Women's Health erosion becomes structural, the high yield equity narrative disappears quickly because the capital structure stops relaxing.

For context, the table below shows where the market is valuing Organon versus a few large and leveraged pharma names on simple headline multiples. The point is not that Organon should trade where Pfizer or Bristol-Myers trades. The point is that the market is already imposing a distinct penalty for leverage and portfolio mix, and that makes the debate narrower: whether the current multiple already assumes enough cash-flow erosion, or whether it is over-discounting a business that still has time to de-lever.

Company TTM P/E TTM EV / EBITDA Organon & Co. 10.6x 6.4x Pfizer Inc. 20.2x 13.1x Bristol-Myers Squibb Co. 17.6x 10.8x Perrigo Company plc N/M 8.8x

Read this table as a pricing map, not a scorecard. Pfizer and Bristol-Myers trade at materially higher enterprise multiples because their balance sheets and portfolio breadth offer more margin for error. Organon's multiple sits far lower because the market is assuming that the cash engine is either shrinking or fragile, and because leverage compresses the equity's tolerance for disappointment. If you believe free cash flow is durable enough to keep chipping away at debt through the 20282031 maturities, the equity's implied cash yield is not just a statistic; it is the mechanism for owner returns. If you believe the cash stream is melting faster than leverage can fall, the low multiple is not protection, it is a warning label.

Institutional Lens

One ownership signal stands out more than the rest of Organon's shareholder register: the presence of Kahn Brothers (Trades, Portfolio) Group, a firm whose investment lineage traces directly to the earliest generation of value investing.

Kahn Brothers (Trades, Portfolio) was founded by Irving Kahn, a long-time practitioner of Benjamin Graham's investment philosophy and one of the last investors directly connected to the original Columbia value-investing tradition. The firm's approach has historically emphasized purchasing securities at a discount to intrinsic value with a clear margin of safety and a willingness to tolerate temporary volatility if the underlying asset value remains intact.

That framework helps explain why Organon appears in the portfolio. According to recent regulatory filings summarized by GuruFocus, Kahn Brothers (Trades, Portfolio) significantly increased its position in Organon, adding roughly 926,000 shares in a recent reporting period and bringing total ownership to approximately 2.49 million shares with a value of more than $37 million. The size of the increase is notable because the firm typically expands positions only when it believes market prices diverge materially from normalized business value.

Seen through that lens, Organon resembles the type of investment situation that often attracts Graham-influenced investors: a company producing meaningful cash flow but carrying a balance sheet that compresses the equity valuation. In these circumstances, the investment thesis does not require rapid growth or a dramatic strategic pivot. Instead, it rests on whether the company can preserve enough cash generation for long enough that leverage declines and the market's perception of risk moderates.

The broader shareholder base reinforces that interpretation. Quantitative and multi-strategy funds have also built positions in the stock, suggesting that Organon screens attractively on systematic factors such as low enterprise valuation and high implied free-cash-flow yield. The overlap between traditional value capital and quantitative models often appears in situations where the equity price is being driven more by balance sheet concerns than by operating performance.

Taken together, the ownership structure reflects a stock that sits at the intersection of deep value and capital-structure optionality. There is little evidence of long-duration growth investors underwriting revenue expansion. Instead, the shareholder base appears aligned around a narrower thesis: that Organon's free cash flow can remain durable long enough for leverage to decline and for equity value to emerge primarily through balance sheet repair rather than through multiple expansion.

Structural Risks

The most important risk in owning Organon is that the balance sheet magnifies any disappointment in the cash engine. Year-end net leverage was disclosed around 4.3x, which is manageable when EBITDA is stable but unforgiving if erosion accelerates. In that structure, the equity does not need a recession to get hurt; it only needs a few quarters where pricing pressure, loss of exclusivity, or access disruption pushes the cash base lower than expected. The company has also guided to a modest step down in adjusted gross margin in 2026, which signals that margin pressure is not only a historical issue but part of the near-term operating reality.

The second risk is that decline in the Established Brands portfolio is not linear. In 2025 the company quantified meaningful loss-of-exclusivity headwinds and pointed to products like Atozet as examples of how quickly revenue can be impaired once exclusivity rolls off. It also continues to describe China's volume-based procurement framework as a structural factor that can widen over time across more products. Owners should not underwrite Established Brands as a stable annuity; they should underwrite it as an amortizing asset where the slope can steepen due to policy, tender dynamics, or clinical guideline shifts.

The third risk is that the growth engine is narrower than it looks. Biosimilars grew in 2025, but management explicitly characterized older molecules such as Renflexis and Ontruzant as mature assets facing significant competitive pricing pressure, with steep declines already visible. That is the business model: portfolio refresh must outpace price compression. If launches and new assets do not replenish faster than older products decay, biosimilars becomes a treadmill rather than a stabilizer.

A fourth risk is that Women's Health durability is partly administrative. Organon attributed a meaningful U.S. Nexplanon decline in 2025 to policy-related access restrictions, which is a reminder that even clinically entrenched products can be disrupted by reimbursement and channel mechanics. The company also divested the JADA system in early 2026, removing a small but real growth offset that had been helping cushion declines elsewhere. That increases the dependence on the remaining Women's Health base to hold steady.

Finally, governance and internal controls have become a real cost of capital issue. The board disclosed the results of an internal investigation into Nexplanon wholesaler sales practices and a resulting leadership transition. Even if the direct financial impact is described as limited, it matters because the investment case depends on investor confidence in reported trends, clean comparability, and disciplined capital allocation. In a leveraged equity, credibility is not optional; it is part of the underwriting.

Conclusion

The core of the Organon thesis is not that the business will surprise on growth, but that it does not need to. At the current valuation, the equity is priced as if the cash engine is already in structural decline and the balance sheet remains a permanent constraint. That framing leaves little room for outcomes where the business simply proves more stable than feared.

What the analysis ultimately reduces to is a question of durability. If adjusted EBITDA holds in a relatively tight range and free cash flow remains anchored around the current level, the company does not need a rerating to create equity value. The path is mechanical: cash is generated, debt is reduced, and the residual claim on the business becomes incrementally more valuable over time. In that scenario, the return is driven by balance sheet compression rather than multiple expansion.

That makes this a deliberately asymmetric situation. The downside is clear, if the Established Brands portfolio erodes faster than expected or pricing pressure accelerates across biosimilars, the leverage that makes the equity interesting becomes a constraint very quickly. But if the current cash profile proves even moderately resilient, the market is already discounting that outcome more heavily than the underlying economics suggest.

On balance, the opportunity sits on the side of that asymmetry. At today's price, the market is valuing Organon as a business in decline with limited time to repair its balance sheet. The evidence points to a narrower outcome: a stable, if unexciting, cash generator with a defined path to deleveraging. If that path holds, the equity does not need to be re-rated to work it simply needs time.

title: Quotient Therapeutics Announces Collaboration with Merck to Discover Novel Drug Targets in Inflammatory Bowel Disease Using Somatic Genomics Platform Technology
content:
Quotient Therapeutics, Inc.

CAMBRIDGE, Mass., March 24, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Quotient Therapeutics, a company pioneering somatic genomics to inform breakthrough medicines, today announced that it has entered a multi-year research collaboration agreement with Merck, known as MSD outside the US and Canada, to discover novel drug targets in inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) using Quotient’s somatic genomics platform technology.

Somatic genetic mutations naturally accumulate over a lifetime, resulting in trillions of different genomes within an individual. Through its platform, Quotient interrogates patient tissue for these mutations within the context of disease to find mutations that cause or protect from that disease. These insights can inform novel therapeutic strategies for a broad range of diseases, including IBD.

“The combination of Merck’s deep drug development and global commercial expertise with our unbiased, disease- and modality-agnostic somatic genomics platform could redefine how IBD is understood and treated,” said Rahul Kakkar, MD, President and Chief Executive Officer of Quotient. “We are proud to work alongside the world-class team at Merck, broadening the reach of our somatic genomics platform and bringing potentially breakthrough therapies to the patients in need.”

“Quotient’s platform has the potential to provide us with unique biological insights into genomic changes that are naturally occurring within patients with IBD. Millions of people globally are living with this disease, and no disease-modifying treatments are currently available,” said Dr. Marc Levesque, Vice President, Discovery, Merck Research Laboratories. “Immunology is a key area of focus for Merck, and we look forward to collaborating on this novel approach to target discovery to help us develop more effective therapeutic options for patients.”

Under the terms of the agreement, Quotient will receive an upfront payment of $20 million. Quotient is also eligible to receive development, regulatory and commercial milestones for a potential total deal value of up to $2.2 billion.

About Quotient

Quotient Therapeutics develops breakthrough medicines informed by natural somatic genetic diversity present in patients. Through our integrated somatic genomics and computational technologies, we gain unbiased, unprecedented resolution into disease-causal drug targets that we leverage to design and develop first-in-class therapies. With this, we are forging a new status quo for biopharma research and development through a broad pipeline of internal and partnered programs. We were founded by Flagship Pioneering in 2022. For more information, visit www.quotient-tx.com.

title: Merck (MRK) Exceeds Market Returns: Some Facts to Consider
content:
In the latest close session, Merck (MRK) was up +1.31% at $115.68. The stock's change was more than the S&P 500's daily gain of 1.15%. At the same time, the Dow added 1.38%, and the tech-heavy Nasdaq gained 1.38%.

Coming into today, shares of the pharmaceutical company had lost 6.61% in the past month. In that same time, the Medical sector lost 8.2%, while the S&P 500 lost 5.69%.

The upcoming earnings release of Merck will be of great interest to investors. The company's earnings report is expected on April 30, 2026. The company is predicted to post an EPS of -$0.57, indicating a 125.68% decline compared to the equivalent quarter last year. Our most recent consensus estimate is calling for quarterly revenue of $16.01 billion, up 3.09% from the year-ago period.

MRK's full-year Zacks Consensus Estimates are calling for earnings of $5.47 per share and revenue of $66.68 billion. These results would represent year-over-year changes of -39.09% and +2.57%, respectively.

It is also important to note the recent changes to analyst estimates for Merck. These revisions help to show the ever-changing nature of near-term business trends. Therefore, positive revisions in estimates convey analysts' confidence in the business performance and profit potential.

Our research shows that these estimate changes are directly correlated with near-term stock prices. To utilize this, we have created the Zacks Rank, a proprietary model that integrates these estimate changes and provides a functional rating system.

The Zacks Rank system, spanning from #1 (Strong Buy) to #5 (Strong Sell), boasts an impressive track record of outperformance, audited externally, with #1 ranked stocks yielding an average annual return of +25% since 1988. Over the past month, there's been a 0.18% rise in the Zacks Consensus EPS estimate. Merck is currently sporting a Zacks Rank of #3 (Hold).

In terms of valuation, Merck is currently trading at a Forward P/E ratio of 20.89. This expresses a premium compared to the average Forward P/E of 14.11 of its industry.

We can additionally observe that MRK currently boasts a PEG ratio of 2.08. The PEG ratio bears resemblance to the frequently used P/E ratio, but this parameter also includes the company's expected earnings growth trajectory. The average PEG ratio for the Large Cap Pharmaceuticals industry stood at 2.08 at the close of the market yesterday.

The Large Cap Pharmaceuticals industry is part of the Medical sector. This industry, currently bearing a Zacks Industry Rank of 176, finds itself in the bottom 29% echelons of all 250+ industries.

title: Can Anktiva's Label Expansion Unlock the Next Growth Phase for IBRX?
content:
ImmunityBio’s IBRX growth narrative is increasingly tied to the expansion potential of its sole marketed drug, Anktiva, beyond its initial approval in BCG-unresponsive non-muscle invasive bladder cancer (NMIBC). While the current label establishes a commercial foothold, the company is advancing multiple clinical programs aimed at extending the therapy’s use across broader oncology indications.

ImmunityBio is evaluating Anktiva in several clinical studies spanning solid tumors and hematologic malignancies as part of its broader Cancer BioShield platform strategy. Under this approach, the therapy acts as a backbone immunotherapy designed to stimulate immune cells such as natural killer (NK) cells and T cells.

Within bladder cancer, IBRX is advancing a randomized study evaluating Anktiva plus BCG in BCG-naïve NMIBC patients — a significantly larger population than the currently approved setting. The company is targeting a potential regulatory filing in the near term, with additional studies exploring the therapy across other disease settings that could further expand its addressable market.

Beyond bladder cancer, ImmunityBio is exploring Anktiva in combination with standard-of-care therapies and CAR-NK approaches across several difficult-to-treat cancers, including non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), pancreatic cancer, glioblastoma, colorectal cancer and hepatocellular carcinoma.

Successful development across the above -mentioned clinical studies could position Anktiva as a foundational immunotherapy platform for multiple cancer types, potentially supporting additional regulatory filings and long-term growth if clinical outcomes remain encouraging.

IBRX Faces Intense Competition in Target Markets

ImmunityBio faces stiff competition from Big Pharma in its target markets.

The company competes with well-established immunotherapies such as Merck’s MRK Keytruda, Bristol Myers’ BMY Opdivo and Roche’s RHHBY Tecentriq. These drugs are already widely used across several oncology indications, including those targeted by IBRX.

Beyond having approved therapies, these pharma giants possess significantly greater financial resources, extensive global commercial infrastructure and well-established supply chains. Their long-standing presence in oncology gives them deep clinical development experience and strong relationships with physicians and treatment centers. This could make market penetration more challenging for newer entrants like ImmunityBio.
